attributes.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of a painless swelling in her neck. She does not recall any incidents of fever, pain, or infections around the swelling. She has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and underwent a colectomy 15 years ago for colon cancer. She takes metformin and captopril. On examination, the swelling is a 2 cm firm, mobile, and non-tender nodule that is superficial, with the skin not moving over the lump. There are no other abnormal findings on examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has a clearly visible, discrete, and well-circumscribed lump in the neck. It is very superficial and most likely to be an epidermoid cyst. These are common benign growths that can be seen in healthy individuals without any underlying medical conditions. These cysts can be found anywhere on the body, but occur mainly on the trunk. Very occasionally, epidermoid cysts can be multiple, such as in the rare inherited condition of Gardner syndrome. This syndrome is a subtype of familial adenomatous polyposis, where the patient presents with multiple colorectal polyps and can also have cutaneous manifestations that include epidermoid cysts, fibromas, and lipomas.

A branchial cyst is a congenital neck mass that is a remnant of the incompletely obliterated 2nd branchial cleft. Although it is considered to be a mass located lateral to the midline, branchial cysts are diagnosed either early in childhood or in adulthood after they have been infected. Because the patient does not present with any symptoms of fever or infections, this is unlikely.

Virchow's node is a metastasis of gastric cancer cells occurring in the left supraclavicular lymph node. Although the location of the patient's mass is near the supraclavicular fossa, Virchow's node is unlikely due to the absence of gastric cancer findings such as weight loss, chronic iron deficiency anaemia, dysphagia, epigastric pain, and haematemesis.

A lipoma is a slow-growing round, rubbery rather than firm swelling that is typically found on the trunk. Whilst lipomas can be found at any site in the body, the neck is a less common site. In addition, a lipoma arises in the subcutaneous tissues and would not be as well defined as the swelling visible in this patient.

A neurofibroma is a benign tumour that usually grows in multiple sites and on the nerves in the body. It can be found in cutaneous or subcutaneous forms. It is mainly found in patients with Neurofibromatosis 1 or 2 (NF 1 or 2). Solitary neurofibromas can be found in patients who do not have NF and typically are less than 2 cm and have a characteristic "buttonhole sign" which is not found in this patient.
learningPoints: Epidermoid cysts are benign, painless and firm tumors that grow superficially on the skin and are associated with Gardner syndrome, a subvariant of familial adenomatous polyposis.
Gardner syndrome is a genetic disorder characterized by the presence of multiple colorectal polyps, osteomas, and soft tissue tumors such as epidermoid cysts.
references: Bisgaard ML, Bülow S. Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP): genotype correlation to FAP phenotype with osteomas and sebaceous cysts. Am J Med Genet A. 2006 Feb 1;140(3):200-4. doi: 10.1002/ajmg.a.31010. PMID: 16411234. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16411234/

LaRiviere CA, Waldhausen JH. Congenital cervical cysts, sinuses, and fistulae in pediatric surgery. Surg Clin North Am. 2012 Jun;92(3):583-97, viii. doi: 10.1016/j.suc.2012.03.015. PMID: 22595710. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22595710/

Zdilla MJ, Aldawood AM, Plata A, Vos JA, Lambert HW. Troisier sign and Virchow node: the anatomy and pathology of pulmonary adenocarcinoma metastasis to a supraclavicular lymph node. Autops Case Rep. 2019 Feb 25;9(1):e2018053. doi: 10.4322/acr.2018.053. PMID: 30863728; PMCID: PMC6394356. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6394356/

Hosny, H., & Farag, A. 2024. Examination of the Head and Neck. In Clinical Surgical Skills Made Easy (pp. 219-234). Cham: Springer Nature Switzerland. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-69158-4_23

Staser K, Yang FC, Clapp DW. Pathogenesis of plexiform neurofibroma: tumor-stromal/hematopoietic interactions in tumor progression. Annu Rev Pathol. 2012;7:469-95. doi: 10.1146/annurev-pathol-011811-132441. Epub 2011 Nov 7. PMID: 22077553; PMCID: PMC3694738. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22077553/
